question.text: What was a key finding in the Paytm Payments Bank case regarding third-party KYC?

answer.text: The bank relied on a non-compliant and unregulated entity for third-party KYC in violation of PMLA rules
The bank conducted excessive KYC beyond what was required
The bank refused to accept third-party KYC from other banks
The bank shared KYC data with unauthorized third parties

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Paytm Payments Bank was found to have relied on a non-compliant/unregulated entity for third-party KYC, violating Section 12 of PMLA read with Rule 9(2)(c) and Rule 9(2)(f). Third-party KYC reliance is permitted only when the third party is a regulated and compliant entity.
**The Trap:** Third-party KYC reliance is allowed under PMLA rules, but only from COMPLIANT and REGULATED entities. Using an unregulated entity for KYC reliance defeats the entire purpose of the KYC framework.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Third-party KYC reliance appears in both IFSCA-01 (Chapter VI of IFSCA Guidelines) and Series XXIV. In IFSC, reliance on third-party KYC from international entities adds another layer of complexity.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When relying on third-party KYC: ensure the third party is (i) regulated, (ii) compliant with AML/CFT requirements, and (iii) able to provide CDD information promptly. The Paytm case shows what happens when you rely on non-compliant parties.
